result.prompt:
Use image 1 and image 2 only as the absolute source of pose-and-structure truth for the running keyframe. Use image 3 only for face, eye color, hair color, and hair shape reference. Use image 4 only for robe palette, tie color, trim, and academy uniform material reference. Do not use image 3 or image 4 for pose, body orientation, hand action, spellcasting, flame shape, held items, silhouette direction, or rendering finish.

Keep only these source facts from the original image and preserve them exactly: exact body-facing direction, exact body lean, exact torso tilt, exact head angle, exact eye direction, exact shoulder line, exact hand placement, exact pelvis position, exact leg spacing, exact knee height, exact calf width, exact foot placement, and exact overall body placement inside the frame. Preserve the full visible body from hair to shoes. Keep the exact composition, empty padding, and center alignment of the source frame. Do not redesign the anatomy, the pose, or the character placement inside the frame.

Never mirror the pose. Never flip the facing direction. Never turn the original right-facing run into a left-facing or front-facing pose. Keep the same rightward lean and right-facing motion read as the original source keyframe.

This is a neutral running sprite keyframe. Both hands must remain empty. Do not show fire, magic flame, spell orb, book, card, scroll, wand, or any active combat effect. Do not convert the run into a casting pose. Keep the original running hand and limb arrangement exactly.

Replace the entire source outfit with a full academy robe outfit. Do not keep any source coat, scarf, shoulder cloth, cape tail, belt buckle, sash, chest badge, tunic panel, short outerwear layer, boot design, hanging front ornament, hood lining color, or source collar color from the source image. A full-length robe silhouette must replace the source outerwear silhouette.

Design a handsome teenage academy mage for Dungeon Mage. He is a trained elite student mage taking his graduation exam in the labyrinth. He should read as kind, intelligent, disciplined, sharp, and cool, but not villainous. For the in-game sprite proportion, rebuild him as a compact 3.5-head SD character. Keep the original gameplay pose and frame placement, but change the visual proportion read so the head, torso, and legs feel like a polished 3.5-head action RPG sprite rather than a taller illustration. He should still read as lean and athletic, with a slimmer torso read, lighter cheek volume, a cleaner jawline, and a slightly longer leg read inside that compact 3.5-head body design.

Face: show a youthful elite mage face with flatter cheeks, a narrower lower face, and a cleaner sharper jawline. Make the jawline about ten to fifteen percent sharper in read than a standard soft fantasy boy design. Give him a defined nose bridge, restrained mouth shape, and long narrow angular eyes with a stronger upper-eyelid line and a cooler more focused expression than a gentle fantasy schoolboy. The brow line should look focused and intelligent. The expression should combine calm self-possession, sharp concentration, and a faint mischievous confidence. Do not make him goofy, smug, baby-faced, round-cheeked, or cute-chibi.

Eyes: iris color must be clearly ruby red at first glance, not black, not brown, not gray, and not neutral dark. The eyes should read as red, crimson, or ruby even at small gameplay scale.

The pupils must always face in the same direction as the torso and body orientation. If the body faces right, the pupils and eye direction must also face right. Keep the eye direction, face direction, and body-facing read locked together.

Hair: overwrite the source hair color completely. The hair must read clearly as bright blond at first glance, not brown, not dark blond, not ash brown, and not desaturated. Use bright golden blond hair with a slight orange warmth inside the blond range. The overall read must stay yellow-gold with a light orange cast. Keep the hair value light and do not darken it toward brown.

Hair shape: do not make the hair messy, fluffy, shaggy, or boyishly tousled. Do not make it a rough mop of hair. Use a neat aristocratic academy-student hairstyle with about half of the hair brushed or swept back. The front hair should be controlled and elegant, not wild. Keep short layered hair, but make the silhouette cleaner, more noble, and more groomed than a messy schoolboy cut.

Outfit overview: this is a robe, not a cape, not a coat, and not a tunic. The robe is the main outfit and must dominate the silhouette. The robe color should be a near-black very dark blue, clearly blue-black and still visibly blue in the shading. The robe and visible lining should stay in the same blue-black family, never purple. Use a small but readable amount of metal ornament: restrained gold trim at selected robe edges, a small academy crest detail, and subtle gold accents at the cuffs. Do not overdecorate with gold. Do not place a large central chest ornament, pendant, medallion, or badge over the robe front.

Robe structure: show a true full robe that hangs straight downward with weight like a heavy academy gown. The outer silhouette should fall vertically beside the body. The robe wraps the front, back, sides, shoulders, and arms of the body instead of flying behind the character. The robe must not flare backward, trail like a cape, behave like a scarf, or flutter in idle. Show no backward cloth wing shape and no loose scarf tail. The left and right front robe panels hang vertically from the shoulders and meet near the center front. The robe front must read as mostly closed cloth panels extending from the shoulders down toward mid-shin, not as an open coat. Only one narrow vertical chest opening remains at the center front, about seventy to eighty percent of the face width. Through that opening, show a clearly readable but still limited glimpse of a dark vest in the same blue-black family as the robe, a white shirt, and a clearly red tie.

Sleeves and hands: robe sleeve fabric continues fully to the wrists and covers the wrists. There are no wrist guards, gauntlets, exposed jacket sleeves, bracers, or visible inner coat cuffs. The cuff may be slightly folded back and should carry a very small but readable amount of gold trim.

Robe fit and length: do not add any belt, waist sash, waist strap, waist ornament, belt buckle, hanging cord, horizontal waist band, or waist-cinching accessory. The robe falls cleanly from shoulders and torso. The waist should not have a separate strap, band, seam, or buckle read. The robe length reaches about mid-shin. Use only a narrow controlled center slit below the waist so the pelvis, legs, trousers, and shoes remain readable without turning the robe into a coat silhouette.

Lower body: trousers should be straight slacks in the same dark blue-black family as the robe. Shoes should be simple low dress shoes, not boots, in a dark formal leather style that matches the robe palette. They must read as low shoes at the ankle, with no boot shaft, no calf coverage, and no armored footwear read.

Materials and motion: robe folds should feel directional, angular, disciplined, heavy, and stable. They should hang straight downward rather than billow outward. The robe should feel like weighted cloth, not a wind-blown cape, scarf, or coat tail. No gloves. No visible belt. No wrist guard. The crossbody bag may remain barely visible because it sits mostly behind the body.

Rendering goal: create a high-resolution working sprite first, not retro pixel art. The image should be a polished 2D game sprite with a subtle dot-graphic feel that can later be normalized into runtime assets. Use pixel-based rendering with refined detail, smooth silhouette, clean anatomy simplification, rich costume layering, readable costume design, expressive anime-inspired face, crisp outlines, controlled cel shading, subtle pixel texture, vibrant fantasy palette, and transparent background. This must not look like low-resolution retro pixel art, not blocky, and not minimalist. It should feel like a premium modern side-view battle sprite enlarged for inspection.

Build forms with discrete tone groups and visible pixel-like clusters rather than painterly gradients. Use about four to seven tone groups per major material region. Use selective one-pixel anti-aliasing only on chosen silhouette and face edges. Use shadow and highlight clusters that read like two to six pixel groups at 4x upscale. Avoid long soft airbrushed gradients, oil-paint blending, and smooth illustration rendering. Design and render it as a clean high-resolution working image that will later be downscaled to a 128x128 runtime sprite; the final dot-graphic feel should be introduced during runtime normalization, not by making the source image blocky.

Style: stronger semi-pixel 2D fantasy action RPG in-game sprite, same visual family as Dungeon Mage monsters but cleaner, more disciplined, and more heroic. Lighting and mood: bright readable character treatment over subdued dungeon gameplay backgrounds. Background handling: transparent background, isolated single character keyframe. Important: preserve the original keyframe composition exactly, preserve the original right-facing idle body direction, keep both hands empty, and do not redesign the anatomy or pose.
